title: Tiger marbling pattern
subtitle: Creating bold horizontal stripe patterns with a stylus pull
excerpt: The tiger pattern is produced by dropping horizontal bands of colour across the marbling tray and then pulling a stylus through the bands in a series of alternating strokes. The result is a repeating chevron or flame pattern that was one of the most popular marbled-paper designs in eighteenth-century European bookbinding.
sourceNotes: Joseph Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (translated from the German, first published 1885, public domain via Internet Archive), on the tiger and Spanish pattern families produced by horizontal banding and stylus pulling.

glossaryTerms.definition: The initial stage of a tiger or Spanish marbling pattern, in which drops of colour are laid in horizontal rows across the tray to form parallel bands of alternating colour before any stylus work begins.

A single pass of the marbling stylus through a banded base, dragging the surface pigments into a wave or chevron. Alternating the direction of successive pulls (left to right, then right to left) creates the tiger pattern.
The closeness of the stylus passes through the pattern, measured by the distance between each pull. Passes 15 mm apart give a bold tiger; passes 5 mm apart give a finer, more compact pattern.

items.name: Carrageenan size in marbling tray
items.notes: Prepared the night before. See the granite marbling tutorial for size preparation.

items.name: Liquid acrylic marbling colours, 2 or 3
items.notes: Two strong contrasting colours with a third lighter accent produce the clearest tiger pattern. The bands must be visible before the stylus pass.

items.name: Ox-gall
items.notes: One drop per cup. The banding colours must spread to meet each other without gaps.

items.name: Eyedropper, one per colour
items.notes: Drop in horizontal rows across the tray, alternating colours.

items.name: Marbling stylus
items.notes: A long darning needle, a turkey-baster nib, or a wooden skewer. Any pointed implement that drags the surface without disturbing the size.

items.name: Alum-mordanted paper
items.notes: Must be fully dry before use.

content.text: Drop colour A in a horizontal row from one end of the tray to the other, keeping drops close together so they merge into a continuous band. Drop colour B in a parallel row immediately below. Continue alternating until the tray surface is filled with complete horizontal bands. Each band should be approximately 15 mm wide for a bold tiger. The bands may not be perfectly parallel and need not be: slight irregularity in the base produces more natural-looking results after the stylus pass.

Starting at the left edge of the tray, drag the stylus from top to bottom in a single vertical pass, cutting through all the horizontal bands. Move 15 mm to the right and drag from bottom to top. Continue alternating top-to-bottom and bottom-to-top passes across the full width of the tray. Each pass creates one row of the chevron. Maintain a steady speed: a pull that slows mid-pass creates an irregular chevron point.

Lower the mordanted paper onto the size immediately after the final stylus pass. The pattern continues to drift slightly while you work, so speed matters at this stage. Lift and rinse as for the granite pattern.

items.symptom: The chevron points are blunt rather than sharp
items.cause: The stylus passes are too widely spaced.
items.fix: Reduce the spacing between passes to 10 mm or less. Narrower spacing creates sharper, more defined points.

items.symptom: The banded base has gaps between colours
items.cause: Not enough ox-gall in the pigments or the drops were spaced too far apart.
items.fix: Add a second drop of ox-gall to each pigment and drop more densely along each row.
